coredata.dc:title: Microwave synthesis of composite of AgInS2 quantum dots and zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 for application in white light emitting diodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Ting
Li, Youpeng
Guo, Chunxian
Ye, Changqing
Chen, Yuanhong
Xie, Zhixiang
coredata.dc:description: Preparation of AgInS2 quantum dots (AIS QDs) with high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and thermal stability remains an important challenge. In this paper, AIS QDs were successfully introduced into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Due to the effective isolation of the ZIF-8, the AIS QDs avoid the concentration quenching when they were assembled into white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) devices as color conversion materials. More importantly, the generation of ZnS layer between the interfaces of AIS QDs and ZIF-8 can efficiently improve the optical performance of AIS QDs, resulting in the fluorescence lifetime extended to 470.05 ns. Meanwhile, the PLQY of AIS/ZIF-8 composite reached up 34.30 % when microwave hydrothermal heating at 120 °C for 60 min with AgIn/Zn=1/3, which was higher than that of the pristine AIS QDs as 25.30 %. Finally, the WLED devices were fabricated by combining AIS/ZIF-8 composite fluorescent powder with (Ba, Sr)Si2O2N2:Eu2+ phosphors and blue LED. At 20 mA, the AIS/ZIF-8 based WLED shows the luminous efficiency (LE) of 44.54 lm/W, correlated color temperature (CCT) of 4242 K and color rendering index (CRI) of 89.1. Moreover, the Commission International de L'Eclairage (CIE) was (0.36, 0.35), indicating the WLED emited warm white light. The present strategy aims at enhancing the optical performance of AIS QDs, which also may trigger the development of QDs-based WLED.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AgInS2 quantum dots
Zeolitic imidazolate framework
Microwave hydrothermal method
White light emitting Diodes
Optical performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of AIS/ZIF-8 composite prepared at (a) 60 °C, (b) 90 °C and (c) 120 °C with different AgIn/Zn molar ratios.
(a) TEM image and (b) STEM image of AIS/ZIF-8 composite with AgIn/Zn=1/3, and mapping results of (c) Ag, (d) In, (e) S and (f) Zn.

(a) absorption, (b) photoluminescence spectra, (c) photoluminescence intensity, and (d) photographs under sunlight and ultraviolet light of AIS/ZIF-8 composite prepared at 60-140 °C for 60 min with AgIn/Zn=1/3.
(a) absorption, (b) photoluminescence spectra, (c) photoluminescence intensity, and (d) PLQY of AIS/ZIF-8 composite prepared at 120 °C for 20-100 min with AgIn/Zn=1/3.
FT-IR spectra of AIS/ZIF-8 composite prepared with different AgIn/Zn molar ratios.
(a) XPS survey spectra, (b) Ag 3d, (c) In 3d, (d) S 2p spectra of the AIS QDs and AIS/ZIF-8 composite, (e) Zn 2p and (f) N 1s spectra of the ZIF-8 and AIS/ZIF-8 composite.
Fluorescence lifetime attenuation curves of AIS/ZIF-8 composite prepared with different AgIn/Zn molar ratios.

(a) EL spectra (20 mA), (b) CIE coordinates, (c) EL spectra of AIS/ZIF-based WLED at operating currents from 20 to 320 mA, and the corresponding changes of (d) luminous efficiency, (e) correlated color temperature and (f) color rendering index under different current drives.
Fitting results of fluorescence lifetime attenuation curves of AIS/ZIF-8 composite prepared with different AgIn/Zn molar ratios.

row.entry: Sample with different AgIn/Zn molar ratios

head.ce:title: Microwave synthesis of composite of AgInS2 quantum dots and zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 for application in white light emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Science & Devices, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou, 215009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aInstitute of Materials Science & Devices, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou, 215009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou, 215009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Sciences
Suzhou University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou, 215009, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Ting Chen and Youpeng Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation of AgInS2 quantum dots (AIS QDs) with high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and thermal stability remains an important challenge. In this paper, AIS QDs were successfully introduced into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Due to the effective isolation of the ZIF-8, the AIS QDs avoid the concentration quenching when they were assembled into white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) devices as color conversion materials. More importantly, the generation of ZnS layer between the interfaces of AIS QDs and ZIF-8 can efficiently improve the optical performance of AIS QDs, resulting in the fluorescence lifetime extended to 470.05 ns. Meanwhile, the PLQY of AIS/ZIF-8 composite reached up 34.30 % when microwave hydrothermal heating at 120 °C for 60 min with AgIn/Zn=1/3, which was higher than that of the pristine AIS QDs as 25.30 %. Finally, the WLED devices were fabricated by combining AIS/ZIF-8 composite fluorescent powder with (Ba, Sr)Si2O2N2:Eu2+ phosphors and blue LED. At 20 mA, the AIS/ZIF-8 based WLED shows the luminous efficiency (LE) of 44.54 lm/W, correlated color temperature (CCT) of 4242 K and color rendering index (CRI) of 89.1. Moreover, the Commission International de L'Eclairage (CIE) was (0.36, 0.35), indicating the WLED emited warm white light. The present strategy aims at enhancing the optical performance of AIS QDs, which also may trigger the development of QDs-based WLED.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the excessive use of fossil energy has caused serious pollution to the environment, people urgently seek for environmentally friendly and efficient lighting source. Compared with incandescent and fluorescent lamps, white light emitting diode (WLED) has the advantages of high efficiency, environment friendliness and durability, which make it has become the third generation of lighting technology [1-3]. However, the current commercial WLEDs assembled by yellow phosphors and blue LEDs always emit cold white light due to the lack of red light emission, which will cause insomnia and endocrine disorders for human after prolonged exposure [2]. In addition, the large size of conventional phosphors makes light scattering serious, leading to the low luminous efficiency of traditional WLEDs [4,5]. In recent years, the environmentally friendly multiple QDs, i.e. CuInS2, CuInSe2, CuGaS2, AgInS2, AgInSe2 and AgGaS2 QDs, posing the advantages of nontoxicity, large Stokes shift and anti-photobleaching stability, are attractive materials for applications in WLEDs [6], solar cells [7], photocatalytic reduction [8] and bioimaging fields [9,10]. Meanwhile, the band gap width of multiple QDs can be adjusted by changing the components, which overcomes the drawback of binary QDs only relying on size adjustment, resulting in the increase of the optical adjustable range [11]. The above advantages make them promising candidates for replacing commonly available Cd or Pb based binary QDs in future WLEDs applications. Among them, AIS QDs have become a kind of ideal material for preparing warm white WLED due to their excellent optical properties, i.e. long fluorescence lifetime, high PLQY, large Stokes shifts and tunable wide spectra from green to deep red regions [12-14]. However, AIS QDs have the problem of different reaction rates of metal ions, making the crystal more defective internally [15]. Moreover, the preparation of multiple QDs is more complex than that of binary QDs, which is reflecting in the difficulty to control the particle size, distribution, composition and luminescence properties. Aim at the above problems, the surface coating or fabricating quaternary alloy QDs strategies were proposed to improve the photoluminescence (PL) properties of multiple QDs, but the synthetic procedures are relatively complex and the photoluminescence properties of quaternary alloy QDs are still need to be enhanced greatly [16].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) refer to a class of porous materials consisting of metal ions and organic ligands coordinated in a suitable solution, which are widely used in electrocatalysis, drug delivery, gas storage and separation due to their large surface area, adjustable pore size, good chemical and thermal stability [17-19]. Recently, introducing QDs into MOFs framework to form the core-shell structure composite can show synergistic effects of QDs with MOFs. Li [20] et al. successfully incorporated carbon dots into MOFs by two-step synthesis method. After more than one month of storage, the fluorescence intensity of composite did not change compared to the original carbon dots, indicating that the stability of dots was greatly improved. Ren [21] et al. embedded all-inorganic perovskite QDs into MOF-5 matrix, which fluorescence intensity remained at 79.2 % of the initial fluorescence intensity. Moreover, the composite could be stored for two months, because the matrix structure of MOF-5 can effectively block the aggregation of QDs and avoid fluorescence quenching. ZIF-8 is a member of the family of MOFs that present a crystalline topology like that of zeolites [22]. It has the apertures of 3.4 Å and pore sizes of 11.6 Å with a high specific surface area (1073 m2 g-1) and thermal stability (600 °C) [23-25]. Meanwhile, coating the wide bandgap semiconductors, such as ZnS and ZnSe, on the surface of QDs to form the core/shell structure QDs was proposed as an efficient strategy to enhance the fluorescence property [26-28]. Compared with binary QDs, AIS QDs has narrow band gap width (1.90 eV) and low lattice mismatch with ZnS shell (3∼7 %), which can enlarge bandgap energy and effectively confine charge carriers inside the AIS cores region from being trapped by surface defect states, resulting in the significant improvement of PLQY [29-32]. Zn element in ZIF-8 can be employed as Zn source to generate ZnS shell layer in AIS QDs solution to further passivate the surface defects of AIS QDs. In this study, ZIF-8 is proposed to encapsulate AIS QDs by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Moreover, the AIS/ZIF-8 composite can avoid the fluorescence quenching caused by the aggregation of AIS QDs and the warming of blue LED [33]. The fluorescence properties of AIS/ZIF-8 composite as a function of microwave temperature, reaction time and AgIn/Zn molar ratio were systematically investigated. Subsequently, the composite was mixed with green commercial phosphor to fabricate WLED devices, which showed luminous efficiency of 44.54 lm/W, color rendering index of 89.1 and correlated color temperature of 4242 K. Compared to the pristine AIS QDs, the AIS/ZIF-8 composite shows enhanced photoluminescence properties and thermal stability simultaneously, indicating that it has a good application prospect in the lighting field.

1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([Bmim]BF4, 98 %) was purchased from Linzhou Keneng Material Technology Co., Ltd and zinc nitrate hexahydrate (99 %) was provided by Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (96 %), imidazole (99.0 %), trisodium citrate hydrate (SC, 99 %), sodium sulfide hydrate (99.8 %), indium nitrate hydrate (99.5 %), silver nitrate (99.8 %) and Silicone were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents (Shanghai, China) Co., Ltd. Glutathione (GSH, 98 %), and 2-methylimidazole (98 %) were provided by Aladdin. All chemicals are used directly without further treatment.

Firstly, 0.0340 g of AgNO3 was added into deionized water to form 0.01 mmol/L precursor solution, and then 0.1909 g of In(NO3)3·4.5H2O and 0.2402 g of Na2S·9H2O were prepared into 0.1 mmol/L precursor solution. Secondly, 0.01 mol and 0.06 mol of AgNO3 and In(NO3)3·4.5H2O were added into the beakers, followed by the addition of organic ligand glutathione powder and trisodium citrate hydrate solution to balance the cationic reaction activity (glutathione/Ag=25 and trisodium citrate hydrate/In=10). Thirdly, deionized water was added and stirred continuously to form a uniform solution (0.6 mmol/L). After that, 0.0791 g of [Bmim]BF4 was added into the solution as the microwave absorbent and surfactant [34], followed by adjusting the pH of the solution to 10 using 4 mol/L NaOH solution. Finally, 1.4 mL of S precursor solution was added, followed by microwave hydrothermal heating at 120 °C for 5 min. The AIS QDs solution was centrifuged with isopropyl alcohol.

Firstly, 2.5656 g of 2-methylimidazole was added to 25 mL deionized water to form 1.25 mol/L uniform solution. Subsequently, 2 mL of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O solution (0.31 mol/L) was added into 2-methylimidazole solution and stirred in a water bath at 30 °C for 30 min. Finally, it was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min and then vacuum dried in an oven at 60 °C for 4 h.

ZIF-8 powder was introduced into AIS QDs solution under ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, Due to the capillary pressure, the AIS QDs were penetrated into the pores of ZIF-8. The AIS/ZIF-8 composite was prepared by microwave reaction at 60-140 °C for 20-100 min. After centrifuge washing with isopropyl alcohol, the composite was achieved and then vacuum dried in an oven at 60 °C for 4 h to obtain the powders for fabrication of WLED.

AIS/ZIF-8 composite was mixed with commercial green powder (Ba, Sr)Si2O2N2:Eu2+ and silicone at the mass ratio of 5:1:6. Then, WLED devices were fabricated in combination with blue LED and their performance was measured at a bias current of 20-320 mA.

In order to analyze the phase of the AIS/ZIF-8 composite, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured using Bruker D8 Advance. For the purpose of understanding the structure of the composite, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were tested by FEI Tecnai F20. For study the fluorescence properties of the composite, the photoluminescence spectra and UV-visible absorption spectra were determined by Shimadzu RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer and Shimadzu UV3600 spectrometer, respectively. In order to understand the functional group information of the composite, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by Nicolet iS50. For investigate the elemental valence information, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested using a ThermoFisher/ESCALAM Xi+ spectrometer. The PLQY of QDs was obtained by placing Rhodamine 6G in ethanol solution as reference, the calculation was performed using the following equation [35]:
Q
Y QDs
= Q
Y dye
×
I QDs
I dye
(
n QDs
n dye
)
2
1 −
10
− O
D dye
1 −
10
− O
D QDs
where QDs and dye represent the QDs solution and rhodamine 6G, respectively. I means the integral area of the PL spectrum. n shows the refractive index, and OD denotes the optical density of either the QDs or rhodamine 6G. The fluorescence lifetime of the composite was measured using the Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrometer. OHSP-350M LED Fast-Scan spectrophotometer was used to test the photoelectric performance of the WLED devices at a bias current of 20-320 mA.

The microwave-assisted hydrothermal method was widely used in the synthesis of various QDs [36-38]. Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of AIS/ZIF-8 composite prepared via microwave-assisted hydrothermal method at 60-120 °C with molar ratio of AgIn/Zn in the range of 1:11-10:1. As shown in Fig. 1, when the molar ratio of AgIn/Zn is higher than 1/3, AIS/ZIF-8 composite shows the diffraction peaks at 2θ=26.96°, 46.07° and 52.20°, corresponding to the crystal plane (112), (204) and (312) of the standard card of AIS (JCPDS NO.25-1330) [39]. The diffraction peaks of AIS/ZIF-8 composite have both AIS QDs and ZIF-8 peaks, where 2θ=10.38°, 12.87°, 14.68°, 16.47° and 18.06° correspond to (002), (112), (022), (013) and (222) crystal planes of ZIF-8, respectively, which correspond to the simulated diffraction peaks of ZIF-8, indicating that the crystal structure of ZIF-8 does not change after recombination [40]. In addition, the diffraction peak of AIS/ZIF-8 composite gradually shifts from 26.96° to 28.04° with decreasing the AgIn/Zn molar ratios. Because part of Zn2+ entry into AIS QDs, and the diffraction peak position gradually closes to ZnS (JCPDS NO.05-0566) with the increase of ZIF-8 content [41].

The microstructure of AIS/ZIF-8 composite can be characterized by TEM. Fig. 2 shows the TEM image and energy spectrum mapping results of AIS/ZIF-8 composite. ZIF-8 shows a spherical shape with the size of 76-103 nm. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the parallel fringes with a standard interplanar spacing value of 0.293 nm correspond to the (200) crystal plane of AIS QDs [42], indicating that AIS QDs were embedded into the ZIF-8 matrix. Fig. 2 (b-f) shows the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) of Ag, In, S and Zn elemental mapping of the AIS/ZIF-8 composite. The results show that Ag, In and S elements corresponded to AIS QDs are dispersed on the surface and inside of the ZIF-8 matrix, which has uniformly distribution of Zn elements [43]. Meanwhile, the atomic contents of Ag, In, S and Zn elements are 2.13 %, 14.68 %, 35.26 % and 47.93 %, respectively, which are close to the initial precursor molar ratio of Ag/In/S/Zn=1/6/14/21, indicating that AIS QDs are successfully combined with ZIF-8 by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method.

Fig. 3 shows the absorption spectra, photoluminescence spectra and photographs of AIS/ZIF-8 composite prepared via microwave-assisted hydrothermal method at 60-140 °C for 60 min. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), there is a wide absorption region below the fundamental absorption edge due to the absorption through AgIn antisite defects, which is well known as Urbach absorption tail [44]. As shown in Fig. 3 (b) and (c), with increasing the microwave temperature from 60 °C to 140 °C, the photoluminescence intensity of AIS/ZIF-8 composite gradually increases and reaches the maximum value at 120 °C, followed by falling down with the further increase of temperature. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of AIS/ZIF-8 composite is higher than that of AIS QDs. Because increasing the temperature is conducive to forming ZnS shell, which can localize the charge carriers and passivate the surface defects of AIS QDs, resulting in the improvement of photoluminescence properties [45]. However, the reduction of PLQY with further increase the temperature can be explained by the additional non-radiative recombination centers creating by the excessive thick ZnS layer [46]. In addition, Zn2+ undergoes cation exchange reactions with Ag+ and In3+, leading to an increase in the band gap and a blue shift in the spectra of AIS QDs [47]. Therefore, the luminescence peak gradually shifts from 613 nm to 578 nm. Meanwhile, the luminescence color of the AIS/ZIF-8 composite under ultraviolet light changes from orange-red to yellow-green, as shown in Fig. 3 (d).

Fig. 4 shows the absorption spectra, photoluminescence spectra, evolution of peak position and photoluminescence intensity, and PLQY of AIS/ZIF-8 composite prepared at 120 °C for 20-100 min with AgIn/Zn=1/3. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), AIS/ZIF-8 composite conforms to the absorption spectrum characteristics of multiple QDs [48]. More importantly, the photoluminescence intensity of QDs is enhanced with the assistance of ZIF-8. The PLQY increases from 25.30 % to 34.30 % after heating at 120 °C for 60 min (Fig. 4 (d)). In addition, the photoluminescence spectra and photoluminescence intensity of AIS/ZIF-8 composites prepared at 120 °C for 20-100 min with different AgIn/Zn molar ratios (15, 10, 5, 3, 1, 1/5 and 1/7) are shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, respectively. Among them, the sample with AgIn/Zn=1/3 prepared at 120 °C for 60 min has the highest photoluminescence intensity. The optimal reaction time for AIS/ZIF-8 composite is 20 min with AgIn/Zn=15 and 10 due to the lack of Zn2+ released by ZIF-8 [49]. Subsequently, as the microwave time continues to be prolonged, the size of AIS QDs increases and the surface ligands are partially oxidized, which makes the photoluminescence intensity decreases and the spectrum continues to red-shift [50,51]. The fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum at 60 min with AgIn/Zn=5 and 3 because the Zn2+ content gradually increases with prolonging the reaction time, while reaches the maximum at 80 min with AgIn/Zn=1. Thereafter, the optimal reaction time is 60 min with AgIn/Zn=1/3 due to the more Zn2+ in the QDs solution (Fig. 4 (b) and (c)), and then is shortened to 20 min with AgIn/Zn=1/5 and 1/7 (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2), because extending the microwave time caused the too thick ZnS shell layer to increase the non-radiative path of AIS QDs [52].

Fig. 5 shows the FT-IR spectra of AIS/ZIF-8 composite prepared with different AgIn/Zn molar ratios. The characteristic peak at 1586 cm-1 represent the ring stretching vibration of imidazolate, and the band at 1389 cm-1 corresponds to the C-N stretching vibration of ZIF in composite [53,54]. In addition, the characteristic peaks at 1428 cm-1 and 1308 cm-1 are attributed to C-N stretching vibrations in 2-methylimidazole, and the peaks at 1146 cm-1 and 755 cm-1 are ascribed to in-plane and out-of-plane bending of the imidazolium ring in 2-methylimidazole, respectively [55,56]. It worth noting that the peak at 523 cm-1 corresponds to Zn-S band [57], indicating that AIS QDs successfully recombined with ZIF-8 and formed ZnS shells.

Fig. 6 shows the element valence information of AIS/ZIF-8 composite and the single component, i.e. AIS QDs and ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), AIS/ZIF-8 composite includes several elements of Ag, In, S, Zn, N and C, which are related to AIS QDs and ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. 6 (b-d), taking AIS QDs as an example, two binding energy peaks of Ag 3d at 373.70 eV for Ag 3d3/2 and 367.60 eV for Ag 3d5/2, In 3d3/2 at 452.20 eV and In 3d5/2 at 444.60 eV for In 3d, and S 2p1/2 at 162.45 eV and S 2p3/2 at 161.35 eV for S 2p, indicating that the Ag, In and S valence states are +1, +3 and -2, respectively [58,59]. Meanwhile, the Ag, In and S valence states of the AIS/ZIF-8 composite remain consistent with AIS QDs. As shown in Fig. 6 (e) and (f), Zn 2p in ZIF-8 can split into Zn 2p1/2 (1044.80 eV) and Zn 2p3/2 (1021.75 eV), indicating that the Zn valence state is +2, and remains unchanged after combined with AIS QDs [60]. N 1s splits into three peaks, corresponding to N-C bond at 398.80 eV, N-H bond at 399.35 eV and N=C bond at 400.35 eV [23,61]. In addition, the binding energy peaks of N 1s increase and those of Zn 2p and S 2p decrease after ZIF-8 combines with AIS QDs. More importantly, the binding energy peaks of Ag 3d and In 3d also shift after combination, indicating that the existence of electron transfer between ZIF-8 and AIS QDs, confirming the formation of Zn-S bonds [62].

Fig. 7 shows the fluorescence attenuation curves of AIS/ZIF-8 composite prepared with AgIn/Zn molar ratios in the range of 1 to 1/7. It is presented in Fig. 7 that the fluorescent decay curves can be fitted through double exponential formula
I ( t ) = A 1 exp ( − t / τ 1 ) + A 2 exp ( − t / τ 2 )
, where τ1 and τ2 are two different time constants corresponding to donor-acceptor pair (DAP) recombination and non-radiative recombination, respectively. The results of fitting the curve with the double exponential function are shown in Table 1 . The short life (τ1) is 61.34 ns and the long life (τ2) is 462.69 ns with AgIn/Zn=1. With the increase of the ZIF-8 content, τ1 and τ2 are 126.73 ns and 508.11 ns with AgIn/Zn=1/3, respectively. Meanwhile, the proportion of τ1 decreases from 50.00 % to 30.77 % and the proportion of τ2 increases from 50.00 % to 69.23 %. At the same time, the average lifetime increases from 415.71 ns to 470.05 ns, which is significantly better than the fluorescence lifetime (360.4 ns) of the reported AIS QDs in the same experimental condition [63]. Because AIS QDs are effectively wrapped and isolated by ZIF-8 frame, which avoids the degradation of fluorescence properties caused by the agglomeration of AIS QDs [64]. Meanwhile, the ZnS shell between ZIF-8 and AIS QDs greatly passivates the surface defects of AIS QDs, enhancing the radiation recombination. Continuing to increase the ZIF-8 content, the proportion of τ1 gradually increases to 50.00 %, and the average lifetime of AIS/ZIF-8 composite further increases to 562.42 ns. Because the excessive ZIF-8 thicken the ZnS shell on the surface of AIS QDs, and thus increased the number of non-radiative recombination channels. In addition, the band gap energies of AIS QDs and ZIF-8 are estimated to be 1.90 eV and 5.07 eV respectively [65]. The conduction band (CB) potential phases compared to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) are about -0.50 and -0.83 V respectively, and the corresponding valence band (VB) potential phases are about 1.40 and 4.24 V vs. NHE, respectively [65]. Therefore, there is a large potential difference between them, and the electrons on CB of AIS QDs cannot be transferred to CB of ZIF-8 after photoexcitation [66]. The band gap energy of ZnS is 3.58 eV, and the CB and VB potential phases are -0.54 and 3.04 V vs. NHE, respectively [65]. Meanwhile, it can be seen from Figs. 5 and 6 that Zn-S bond is formed between AIS QDs and ZIF-8 interface, accompanied by electron transfer phenomenon. Therefore, the electrons on CB of AIS QDs are captured by Zn-S bond, thus inhibiting carrier recombination and prolonging the fluorescence lifetime [66,67]. Due to the compositional complexity, AIS QDs have great defects, such as Vs, VAg, VIn, InAg, Ini, and AgIn, which can accommodate large non-stoichiometric quantities in the structure [68-70]. Currently, DAP recombination is the most recognized emission mode for AIS QDs, such as the transition from VS to VAg or from InAg to VAg, in which the InAg or VS acts as the donor and VAg acts as the acceptor [71]. In addition, the radiation recombination mode also relates to the surface defect state. Fig. S3 shows the energy level diagram of AIS QDs and ZnS. When the wide band gap ZnS (3.58 eV) coats on the surface of AIS QDs (1.90 eV), it can well passivate the surface defects of AIS QDs, resulting in the improvement of emission property [72,73].

In order to explore the application prospect of AIS/ZIF-8 composite in the field of WLED, AIS/ZIF-8 composite powders were mixed with commercial phosphor (Ba,Sr)Si2O2N2:Eu2+, and then were coated on blue LED. The parameter changes of the WLED at 20-320 mA were measured, as shown in Fig. 8 . The luminescence peaks at 445 nm and 493 nm correspond to the blue LED and the commercial phosphor, respectively, while the peak at 607 nm belongs to the AIS/ZIF-8 composite. Driving at 20 mA current, AIS/ZIF-8 based WLED shows the color rendering index of 89.1, which is higher than the commercial WLED (color rendering index<75) [12]. The correlated color temperature of the prepared WLED is 4242 K and the CIE is (0.36, 0.35) (Fig. 8 (a) and (b)), indicating that WLED emits bright warm white light. In addition, AIS/ZIF-8 based WLED has the luminous efficiency of 44.54 lm/W, making it promising candidate in the application of WLED field. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 8 (c-f), the EL intensity of the AIS/ZIF-8 based WLED continues to increase as the current increases from 20 mA to 320 mA, which means the AIS/ZIF-8 composite is not saturated [74]. And the luminous efficiency of the device slightly decreases as the current increases, while the correlated color temperature and color rendering index are almost unchanged, indicating that AIS/ZIF-8 based WLED has good stability [75]. Because QDs are prone to concentration quenching and thermal quenching, leading to deterioration of device performance, while the ZIF-8 frame can be effectively isolated the QDs, and the ZnS shell layer greatly reduces the carriers captured by defects, resulting in the improvement of the stability of AIS QDs [76].

In summary, we successfully prepared AIS/ZIF-8 composite with excellent optical properties by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The PLQY of AIS/ZIF-8 composite showed 34.30 % after microwave hydrothermal heating at 120 °C for 60 min with AgIn/Zn molar ratio of 1/3. Due to the generation of Zn-S bond between AIS QDs and ZIF-8 interface acting as the charge capture point, the carrier lifetime was prolonged, resulting in the fluorescence lifetime of AIS/ZIF-8 composite as high as 470.05 ns. Finally, the AIS/ZIF-8 composite was mixed with green phospher and then coated on a blue LED to achieve WLED device. Under 20 mA illumination, the AIS/ZIF-8 based WLED presents high color rendering index of 89.1, and correlated color temperature and luminous efficiency are 4242 K and 44.54 lm/W, respectively, which shows significant prospect in the field of solid-state lighting application.

Ting Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Youpeng Li: Visualization, Validation, Software, Investigation. Chunxian Guo: Software. Changqing Ye: Software. Yuanhong Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhixiang Xie: Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was kindly supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52062019), the Qinglan Project of Jiangsu Province.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.139643.
